title: How the Iran Deal Sets the Stage for More Conflict
gist: Georgetown's Daniel Byman argues the 2026 U.S.-Iran truce is not a settlement but a framework for prolonged low-grade conflict, modeled on the failed 2025 Gaza deal. A newly emboldened post-assassination Iranian leadership, an excluded and spoiler-prone Israel, hedging Gulf states, and alienated allies make a durable agreement implausible; the Strait of Hormuz and toll-extraction emerge as Iran's decisive leverage.

insights.claim: The 2026 U.S.-Iran truce is most likely to produce not a diplomatic breakthrough but a 'violent muddle' of on-and-off, limited violence, because the hard questions on nuclear, missiles, and money were deferred rather than resolved.
insights.speaker: Daniel Byman
insights.so_what: Markets pricing a clean Iran-risk resolution are mispriced; expect recurring flare-ups and headline-driven oil volatility rather than a durable de-escalation.
insights.quote: the most likely outcome is not a breakthrough but a violent muddle, with on-and-off, but often limited, violence.
insights.locator: That's the nature of truce deals

insights.claim: The most instructive parallel for the Iran truce is the 2025 Gaza peace plan, not the 2015 JCPOA: a deal that generated optimistic headlines but functioned only as a framework for continued limited conflict rather than a true settlement.
insights.speaker: Daniel Byman
insights.so_what: Use the Gaza-deal template, not the JCPOA, to forecast the Iran path: announcement euphoria followed by unimplemented promises and persistent low-grade violence.
insights.quote: The most instructive parallel is not the Joint Comprehensive Plan of Action (JCPOA)... but the 2025 Gaza peace plan.

insights.claim: None of Trump's 20-point Gaza peace-plan promises—reconstruction, demilitarization, an international stabilization force—have been implemented, and Israeli operations have killed more than 1,000 Palestinians in Gaza since the October cease-fire without a return to all-out war.
insights.speaker: Daniel Byman
insights.so_what: The Gaza precedent shows post-deal 'peace' can coexist with ongoing mass casualties and aid disruption, a base case for Iran-deal follow-through.
insights.quote: Israeli operations have killed more than 1,000 Palestinians in Gaza, with Israel at times curbing or halting aid deliveries.

insights.claim: The most honest forecast for the Iran conflict is that it will continue at varying levels, in one form or another, for a long time, because vague language cannot bind actors with divergent interests and minimal mutual trust.
insights.speaker: Daniel Byman
insights.so_what: Treat Middle East risk as a structural, persistent input to energy and geopolitical exposure rather than an episodic shock that resolves.
insights.quote: This conflict will continue at varying levels, in one form or another, for a long time.

insights.claim: Iran's demonstrated ability to close the Strait of Hormuz and strike U.S. Gulf allies is a strategic game-changer that lets Tehran coerce neighbors and impose global economic costs on the United States.
insights.speaker: Daniel Byman
insights.so_what: Hormuz closure risk is now a credible, repeatable Iranian tool—price persistent tail risk into oil, tanker, and shipping-insurance exposure.
insights.quote: Iran's abilities to strike U.S. allies in the Gulf and close the Strait of Hormuz, however, are game-changers.
insights.locator: At the same time, Iran's use of proxies

insights.claim: Iran can extract tolls on Hormuz traffic—whether branded as transit fees or maritime-security arrangements—which provide direct relief from the U.S. economic pressure that was Washington's primary pre-war weapon against Tehran.
insights.speaker: Daniel Byman
insights.so_what: Watch for new Hormuz transit fees as a sanctions-relief workaround; they would lift Iran's revenue and raise the cost basis for crude shipped through the strait.
insights.quote: Whatever euphemism is used (transit fees, maritime security arrangements, etc.), these provide relief from U.S. economic pressure.

insights.claim: The cease-fire terms reportedly offer Iran far more financial relief than the Obama-era JCPOA, with no safeguards written in on enrichment, stockpiles, or fund diversion to proxies.
insights.speaker: Daniel Byman
insights.so_what: Unsafeguarded fund release raises proxy-rearmament and renewed-conflict odds, undercutting the deal's durability premium.
insights.quote: The outlines of the agreement suggest that the United States is offering far more financial relief than was done under the Obama administration.

insights.claim: Iran's new post-assassination leadership has absorbed the lesson that the United States only understands force and that Iran must and can stand up to it, a sharp break from Khamenei's hostile-but-cautious posture.
insights.speaker: Daniel Byman
insights.so_what: A more confrontational Tehran raises the probability of provocative moves (Hormuz disruption, allied strikes), increasing geopolitical risk premia.
insights.quote: that the United States only understands force and that Iran must and can stand up to it.
insights.locator: The new leadership has absorbed a different lesson

insights.claim: Under Khamenei, Iran declined to close Hormuz even under severe pressure during Operation Midnight Hammer in June 2025, illustrating how much the leadership's risk tolerance has since changed.
insights.speaker: Daniel Byman
insights.so_what: The old red line on Hormuz no longer holds; the deterrent baseline has shifted toward greater willingness to escalate.
insights.quote: Iran was unwilling, for example, to close Hormuz during Operation Midnight Hammer in June 2025, even under severe pressure.
insights.locator: The previous leadership under Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei

insights.claim: Iran's negotiating behavior will be slow, opaque, and prone to sudden reversals because the new leadership is still consolidating power and internal communication is impaired by counterintelligence fears after the assassination campaign.
insights.speaker: Daniel Byman
insights.so_what: Expect protracted, reversal-prone talks; do not extrapolate near-term diplomatic progress from any single round.
insights.quote: Negotiations conducted under those conditions are likely to be slow, opaque, and prone to sudden reversals.
insights.locator: This attitude makes Tehran more intransigent

insights.claim: Iran's proxy network has flipped from leverage to liability—with Hezbollah and Hamas devastated, Iran must now protect Hezbollah rather than wield it as a forward weapon.
insights.speaker: Daniel Byman
insights.so_what: The degraded Axis of Resistance shifts Iran toward direct tools (Hormuz, Gulf strikes), changing the channels through which regional risk transmits.
insights.quote: Iran must protect Hezbollah, not the other way around—rather than leverage.
insights.locator: At the same time, Iran's use of proxies

insights.claim: Iranian distrust is structural: Tehran watched the U.S. exit the JCPOA despite IAEA-certified compliance and then use 2025 negotiations as cover for an Israeli attack, breeding a short-term mentality and skepticism that any deal will last.
insights.speaker: Daniel Byman
insights.so_what: Deep distrust caps the achievable durability of any agreement; verification and good-faith assumptions should be discounted heavily.
insights.quote: Then the United States used negotiations in 2025 as cover for an Israeli attack.

insights.claim: Israel was excluded from the truce negotiations and even blocked from reading the deal text before release, despite holding more existential stakes than the United States, creating incentives for it to act as a spoiler.
insights.speaker: Daniel Byman
insights.so_what: An excluded, aggrieved Israel can unilaterally disrupt the deal via strikes on Hezbollah, a key destabilization vector to monitor.
insights.quote: Israel has been excluded from the negotiations (and was even blocked from reading the text of the truce deal before its release).

insights.claim: Netanyahu faces October 2026 Israeli elections with the deal widely perceived domestically as favoring Iran, giving him political incentive to maintain an aggressive military posture.
insights.speaker: Daniel Byman
insights.so_what: The October election window is a concrete near-term catalyst for renewed Israeli escalation against Iranian proxies.
insights.quote: The deal is widely perceived in Israel as favoring Iran, and this reflects poorly on Netanyahu.
insights.locator: Israel will hold elections in October

insights.claim: Israel is establishing a permanent military footprint and buffer zones in Gaza, Lebanon, and Syria, an implicit decision to trade the prospect of peace for strategic depth.
insights.speaker: Daniel Byman
insights.so_what: Permanent occupation posture signals indefinite regional militarization, reinforcing the structural-conflict base case.
insights.quote: It is establishing what appears to be a permanent military footprint and buffer zones in Gaza, Lebanon, and Syria.

insights.claim: Analyst Natan Sachs's 'anti-solutionism' framework—treating the absence of a negotiated settlement as an objective rather than a failure—now governs Israeli strategy across multiple conflicts, using force to set adversaries back rather than to gain negotiating advantage.
insights.speaker: Daniel Byman
insights.so_what: If Israel's goal is permanent non-settlement, deal-making models that assume both sides seek closure will systematically misforecast outcomes.
insights.quote: an approach that treats the absence of a negotiated settlement with the Palestinians not as a failure but as an objective.

insights.claim: Gulf states that depend on U.S. security guarantees watched Washington fail to protect them from Iran's drones and missiles during the war, then proceed to a deal placing no limits on Iranian rearmament, pushing them toward hedging over reliance.
insights.speaker: Daniel Byman
insights.so_what: Gulf hedging away from U.S. guarantees reshapes basing access and regional alignment—watch for diversification toward China and independent security arrangements.
insights.quote: Gulf states that depend on U.S. security guarantees have watched the last year with deep unease.

insights.claim: Qatar made financial payments to Iran in exchange for immunity from attacks, with at least tacit U.S. support, before the cease-fire was finalized.
insights.speaker: Daniel Byman
insights.so_what: Pay-for-immunity precedents normalize Iranian coercion of Gulf wealth, a recurring channel for sanctions-evading inflows to Tehran.
insights.quote: Qatar made financial payments to Iran in exchange for immunity from attacks with at least tacit U.S. support.

insights.claim: Gulf states may deny the U.S. basing and access rights, concluding that hosting U.S. bases makes them more of a target rather than more secure.
insights.speaker: Daniel Byman
insights.so_what: Loss of Gulf basing would degrade U.S. regional force projection, a long-horizon shift in the Middle East security architecture.
insights.quote: concluding that it cannot reliably protect them and that hosting U.S. military bases makes them more of a target rather than more secure.
insights.locator: In the future, Qatar and other Gulf states

insights.claim: European and Asian allies' grievances compound prewar U.S. offenses—tariffs on close allies and talk of seizing Greenland from Denmark—raising durable questions about the reliability of U.S. leadership.
insights.speaker: Daniel Byman
insights.so_what: Cumulative allied distrust outlasts any single deal, a structural drag on U.S. alliance cohesion and coordinated policy.
insights.quote: these build on prewar U.S. offenses, including tariffs on close allies and talk of seizing Greenland from Denmark.
insights.locator: European and Asian allies have their own grievances

insights.claim: U.S. long-term Middle East intentions are deeply uncertain: the Trump administration's 2025 National Security Strategy declared the region 'no longer the constant irritant' even as the latest war suggests continued entanglement.
insights.speaker: Daniel Byman
insights.so_what: Contradiction between stated disengagement and actual involvement creates unpredictable U.S. policy, amplifying region risk.
insights.quote: the Middle East “is no longer the constant irritant, and potential source of imminent catastrophe, that it once was.”

insights.claim: U.S. support for Israel is waning among both Republicans and Democrats, removing a past driver of American involvement in the region.
insights.speaker: Daniel Byman
insights.so_what: Bipartisan erosion of pro-Israel consensus signals a longer-run U.S. policy realignment with downstream effects on aid and diplomatic backing.
insights.quote: support for Israel, one past driver of U.S. involvement in the region, is waning among both Republicans and Democrats.
insights.locator: The United States has no interest in a peace process

insights.claim: The Middle East has a history of defeating minimalist U.S. strategies, so the deal will play out amid U.S. flip-flopping, Israeli operations, Iranian proxy activity, Gulf hedging, and allied frustration.
insights.speaker: Daniel Byman
insights.so_what: Bet against any thesis premised on a tidy, minimal-effort U.S. exit from the region; complexity historically forces re-engagement.
insights.quote: The Middle East, however, has a history of defeating minimalist U.S. strategies.
